narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 53.4% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the decision to reject your application—they use this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed. Your profile shows solid demographics: you're a white male planning full-time studies, which aligns closely with the majority of applicants, and your family's middle-income bracket is fairly typical. However, your academic record stands out in less favorable ways, including a third-decile ranking in your school's Year 1 grades (compared to the average fifth or sixth decile) and a similar third-decile position in Year 3 grades (versus the typical mid-fifth decile). Your LSAT score of 32 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90 are also below the dataset averages of around 37 and 3.21, respectively. In assessing your bar exam odds, the model weighed your Year 3 decile ranking as the most influential factor, as it pushed strongly toward predicting failure since it's lower than what's common for successful students. Your LSAT score came in second, similarly nudging the prediction toward failure due to being under the norm. Your Year 1 decile ranked third in influence, again contributing negatively because of its below-average standing. Family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status followed in descending order of impact, each adding a bit more toward the failure prediction, though with lesser pull—your demographics were neutral or typical, so they didn't help offset the academics. Overall, this made your profile riskier than most applicants, whose stronger grade deciles, LSATs, and GPAs predict better bar passage. The close 53.4% probability means it's not a landslide; small improvements could shift it. To boost your future chances, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim higher than 37, as that's a changeable score that heavily sways predictions. Building better study habits now could also lift future grade deciles if you pursue more schooling, proving upward trends to admissions teams. Keep pushing—you're close, and targeted academic gains can rewrite this story.
